turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I've tried quitting before, but I always end up going back to it. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a huge part of my life, you know? Especially since I work at a pub. It's hard to resist the temptation when I'm drinking with friends and colleagues.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 4 — Planning]

You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.
[Patient Profile]

- Motivation to quit: family pressure, weight concerns
- Known triggers: social situations, evening loneliness
- Tried strategies: quitting before → did not help

- Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

turns.user_message: I don't know... I just got complacent, I guess. I thought I had it beat, but then I started hanging out with friends who smoke, and next thing I knew, I was back to my old habits. And to be honest, I didn't really believe it was that big of a deal. I'm healthy, I exercise, I figure I'll be fine.
step2_rag_chunks: Within weeks of quitting smoking, breathing and circulation often improve.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.

step2_rag_chunks: Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.
